coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent aptasensing assay using carbon dots and Ag-Au nanoparticles for the detection of Pb2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Samimi, Heshmat Allah
Saberi, Zeinab
coredata.dc:description: Lead (Pb) as one of the highly toxic heavy metals poses a threat to human health even at trace levels. The development of sensitive and rapid method to detect Pb2+ is important. Herein, a new biosensor was developed based on fluorescence quenching and ssDNA as a recognition tool for Pb2+ detection. A fluorimetric aptasensor was introduced using carbon dots as fluorophores conjugated to aptamers (CDs-Apt) and Ag@Au nanoparticles (Ag@AuNPs) as a quencher. The interaction between the Ag@AuNPs and CDs-Apt leads to a decrease in the fluorescence signal. By the addition of Pb2+ to the solution, specific interactions between the aptamers and Pb2+ ions cause to detach CDs-Apt from Ag@AuNPs. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity is enhanced proportionally to the Pb2+ concentration. The proposed aptasensor is able to detect Pb2+ in a linear range of 0.5 to 300 nM with 0.2 nM as a detection limit. The aptasensor is able to monitor Pb2+ levels in the real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pb2+ ions
Carbon dots
Ag-Au nanoparticles
Aptamer
Fluorimetric aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV/Vis absorption spectrum; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (C) FT-IR spectrum; (D) H-NMR spectrum of CDs-COOH.
(A) TEM image; (B) UV/Vis absorption spectrum; (C) Histogram of size distribution; (D) FT-IR spectrum of Ag@AuNPs.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of biosensor for different Pb2+ concentrations (0.5–300 nM) under optimized conditions; (B and C) Calibration curve of Pb2+ aptasensor.
Selectivity test of the aptasensor toward Pb2+ and other metal ions Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ with same concentrations (250 nM).
Schematic presentation of the Pb2+ fluorescence aptasensor.
Analytical parameters of this aptasensor compared to other Pb2+ fluorometric sensors.
Results of Pb2+ determination in the real samples using the proposed aptasensor.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent aptasensing assay using carbon dots and Ag-Au nanoparticles for the detection of Pb2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahrekord Branch, Islamic Azad University, Shahrekord, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahrekord Branch, Islamic Azad University, Shahrekord, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shahrekord University, Shahrekord, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead (Pb) as one of the highly toxic heavy metals poses a threat to human health even at trace levels. The development of sensitive and rapid method to detect Pb2+ is important. Herein, a new biosensor was developed based on fluorescence quenching and ssDNA as a recognition tool for Pb2+ detection. A fluorimetric aptasensor was introduced using carbon dots as fluorophores conjugated to aptamers (CDs-Apt) and Ag@Au nanoparticles (Ag@AuNPs) as a quencher. The interaction between the Ag@AuNPs and CDs-Apt leads to a decrease in the fluorescence signal. By the addition of Pb2+ to the solution, specific interactions between the aptamers and Pb2+ ions cause to detach CDs-Apt from Ag@AuNPs. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity is enhanced proportionally to the Pb2+ concentration. The proposed aptasensor is able to detect Pb2+ in a linear range of 0.5 to 300 nM with 0.2 nM as a detection limit. The aptasensor is able to monitor Pb2+ levels in the real samples with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, contamination of environment with heavy metals (such as mercury, cadmium, lead, and etc.) has become a global issue because of rapid industrialization and development [1]. Heavy metals are described by several features including high level of toxicity, water solubility, low degree of degradability, cancer-causing, bio-accumulation and intrinsic persistence. So, the monitoring of poisonous heavy metal ions is vital [2–4]. Lead (II) ions (Pb2+) is considered as one of the most hazardous metallic pollutants. Lead have been commonly applied in a wide range of products such as lead batteries (especially in the car industry), radiation protection, soldering, gasoline, pigments, cosmetics and etc. Considering to long biological half-life compared with other heavy metals and high compatibility with red blood cells, long-time exposure to cadmium over tolerance limits can damage the nervous, blood, digestive, reproductive and kidney systems [5–8]. According to the maximum value of 10 µg/L for lead in drinking-water recommended by WHO guideline, the demand for developing highly sensitive method has been increasing [9]. Conventionally, the most common techniques used to detect Pb2+ rely on expensive instruments with complicated procedures including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10], atomic absorption and emission spectroscopy [11] and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [12]. So, to satisfy the demand for a rapid, sensitive, and economical method, optical assays can be a suitable alternative to the traditional analytical instruments. Among the optical methods, fluorometric assay have been broadly considered for Pb2+ ions analysis due to their sensitivity and reliability [13–15].

In recent years, fluorimetric assay based on aptamer have increasingly become a promising analytical tool in field of analytical analysis due to combining the benefits of fluorescence method with the high selectivity and binding affinity of aptamer [16,17]. Aptamer is a short single-stranded oligonucleotide which can specifically capture its target with high affinity, similar to antigen–antibody interactions [18,19]. The development of fluorometric aptasensors based on nanomaterials has gained much attention due to improve the performance and precision of aptasensors [20]. A variety of fluorescent nanoprobe have been used in aptamer-sensing. Carbon dots (CDs) are the last group of carbon nanomaterials with a size blew 10 ​nm that included a carbon core, and a surface with functional groups, such as amines or carboxylic acid. They have received great popularity thanks to outstanding photoluminescence features, considerable photostability, as well as biocompatibility [21 22].

Metallic nanoparticles including gold, pelathinium and silver nanoparticles (Au, Pt and Ag NPs) have got attention due to optical features such as distance-dependent optical properties and extinction coefficients. Bimetallic nanomaterials exhibit not only properties related to individual metallic nanomaterials but also have more excellent features compared to individual materials, such as enhanced biocompatibility, more stability and dispersion, catalytic activity [23,24]. There are two methods to synthesize bimetallic NPs including the bottom-up and the top-down methods. Ag@Au bimetallic nanomaterials have opened a new field of research owing to more effective nature and wide applications. There are two categories of Ag@Au NPs: alloy NPs and core–shell structure NPs.Recently, these Ag@Au bimetallic nanomaterials can be employed in fluorescence biosensors as a quencher due to their mentioned properties [25,26].

The variations in the fluorescence signal caused by interaction between the analyte and the fluorophore can be based on two processes of “OFF-ON” and “ON-OFF” modes and different mechanism such as inner filter effect (IFE). IFE is a significant mechanism in fluorescence quenching resulting from the spectral overlap of fluorophore’s emission or excitation and the quencher’s absorption spectrum [27,28].

In this work, a new fluorometric aptasensor has been presented to determine the Pb2+ ions, inspired by the unique features of CDs and Ag@AuNPs as a fluorophore and a quencher, respectively. Despite the different successful reports of CDs-based aptasensor for metal sensing, the use of CDs conjugated to aptamer for the fluorometric detection of lead ions has been minimally explored [29]. The sensing principle is based on fluorescence signal quenching caused by interaction between CDs-Apt and Ag@AuNPs in the absence of Pb2+ and recovering of fluorescence intensity in the presence of Pb2+. To our best knowledge, it is the first time to introduce a aptasensor fabricated by CDs and Ag@AuNPs for rapid detection of Pb2+.

Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5%), l-Arginine (Arg, >98%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), Meldrum’s acid, 4-dimethylaminopyridine, cadmium (II) nitrate, potassium nitrate, lead (II) nitrate, cobalt (II) nitrate, nickel (II) nitrate, and chromium (II) chloride hydrate, AuHCl4·3H2O and silver nitrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The aptamer of Pb2+ with sequence of 5′ –NH2- TGGGT GGGT GGGT GGGT-3′ was prepared from Takapu Zist Institute in Iran.

Arg and EDA as carbon and nitrogen sources were used to synthesize CDs using a microwave-assisted treatment. Typically, Arg (87.0 mg), EDA (33.0 μL, 0.5 mmol) and 100 μL of Milli-Q water were mixed in a microwave reaction vessel and next, heated for 180 s at 240 °C. In following, a dark yellow-colored solution was filtered using a 0.1 µm microporous membrane and next, Milli-Q water was used to dialyze solution for 2 days. Finally, it was lyophilized, giving a brownish solid.

The CDs with amino groups on the surface were modified to carboxylic acid through pervious procedure [30]. 23.9 mg of Meldrum’s acid and 2.5 mg of 4-dimethylaminopyridine were added to 25.0 mg of CDs dissolved in anhydrous DMF (5.0 mL). The mixture was stirred under argon at 80 °C for 48 h. After that, the solvent was removed under high vacuum and the residue was dissolved in methanol and the purification was accomplished through size exclusion chromatography. The a brownish solid of CDs-COOH was obtained using lyophilizing.

The CDs-COOH conjugated to aptamer (CDs-Apt) was prepared through carbodiimide condensation reaction of the carboxylic functional group of CDs-COOH with the amino group of aptamers. To buffer solution of 10 mg of CDs, EDC and NHS (1:1, 50 mM) were added under shaking for 60 min. Next, the Pb2+ aptamer was introduced to above solution under shaking for 6 h. Ultimately, the solution was filtered to eliminate unconjugated aptamers through centrifugal filtration (MWCO = 10 kDa) at 4000 rpm for 30 min.

Ag@AuNPs were prepared according to the literature procedure [23]. 309 µL of 30 mM HAuCl4 solution and 50 mL water were heated to boiling. After addition of 198 µL of 20 mM AgNO3 solution, 2.5 mL of sodium citrate (1%) was added to the solution under reflux and heated for 30 min. Finally, the solution was stirred until it was cooled.

The fluorimetric procedure for measuring Pb2+ can be performed as following. 20 µL of CDs-Apt probe solution was diluted in the Tris-HCl buffer pH 7.4 and its fluorescence emission signal was recorded ranging from 340 to 600 nm using the excitation wavelength of 330 nm as a control signal. Afterward, an optimal concentration of Ag@AuNPs was added that caused to decrease the signal intensity during the 10 min. The signal intensity was assigned as F0. Upon addition of different concentration of Pb2+ ions and incubated for 20 min, the fluorescence single defined as F was increased. An analytical signal was defined as the ratio of F to F0 at a wavelength of 402 nm.

Tap water and milk were considered as the real samples to verify the practicality of the biosensor. The milk sample was spiked with varying concentration of Pb2+. Then, samples were mixed trichloroacetic acid (1%,v/v) and centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 20 min to remove the proteins. Afterwards, the above obtained solution and tap water samples were passed from a 0.22 μm filtration membrane [31].

CDs: CDs were synthesized from EDA and Arg and followed by modifying amino groups of CDs to carboxylic acid on the surface. The detailed processes are defined in the Experimental Section. The characterization of CDs including optical properties, the mapping of the surface groups, the morphology and size were explored. Firstly, optical properties of the CDs-COOH were investigated by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the UV–Vis spectrum of CDs-COOH have an absorption peak between 250 and 300 that attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC and CO units. According to emission profile (Fig. 1b), CDs-COOH exhibit the excitation dependent fluorescence that may arise from a distribution of emissive domains and dots with different sizes. The optimal excitation wavelength was chosen 330 nm.
FT-IR and 1H NMR spectroscopies were used for characterization of chemical structure. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs-COOH in Fig. 1C displays the peaks of CO bonds at 1645 cm−1 and.

–OH stretching mode at 3400 cm−1. Moreover, absorptions at 2945 cm−1 and 1430 cm−1 can be related to CH and the symmetric stretching of the carboxylate group, respectively. The conjugation of CDs-COOH to aptamer was showed by the FT‐IR spectrum (Fig. S2). Peaks around 1110, and 985 cm−1 are associated to PO or CO stretching and stretching of CC or CO deoxyribose. 1H NMR spectrum shows the signals related to the carboxylic acid at 12.2 ppm and amide NH at 6.5 ppm (Fig. 1D). Kaiser test confirmed the decrease of the amount of amines from 1350 µmol g−1 in the CDs to 100 µmol g−1 in the CDs-COOH. The size and morphology of CDs-COOH were characterized by AFM. Average size of spherical CDs-COOH were determined about 4.0 nm. The TEM analysis confirms that the size of CDs is around 4.0 nm (Fig. S3).

Ag@AuNPs: The TEM image demonstrates spherical Ag@AuNPs with an average diameter of 13 nm (Fig. 2 A, B). The UV–visible absorption spectrum in Fig. 2C displayed a single absorption band with the maximum wavelength at 422 nm associated to the surface plasmon resonance. The FT-IR spectroscopy was used to explore the surface functional groups of Ag@AuNPs (Fig. 2D). The characteristic bands at 1400 and 1740 cm−1 are corresponding to COO– and CO bonds, respectively. A broad band centered at 3400 cm−1 is related to –OH.

In this work, bimetallic nanomaterials of Ag@AuNPs have been considered as a quencher of fluorometric sensing because of their biocompatibility, high surface to volume ratio, alterable optical properties and strong photostability. According to the overlap between absorption spectrum of Ag@AuNPs and fluorescence spectrum of CDs-COOH in Fig. S4, it is expected that IFE has occurred. The schematic diagram of the principle of Pb2+ aptasensor is displayed in Scheme 1 . In the absence of Pb2+, approaching the G-rich ssDNA labeled with CDs onto the surface of Ag@AuNPs causes fluorescence quenching. Upon addition of Pb2+, ssDNA wrapped around Pb2+ ions with a strong affinity and released from the quencher. As a result, the fluorescence signals were restored.

In order to reach maximum sensing performance, the pertinent experimental variables have been optimized including (i) concentration of the quencher and (ii) interaction time of Ag@AuNPs with CDs-Ap, (iii) incubation time of Pb2+ binding. To get maximum quenching performance, fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ap was explored by varying Ag@AuNPs concentrations. As you can see in Fig. S5A and B, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ap was reduced upon the addition of quencher to 0.20 mg/mL of Ag@AuNPs that the fluoresce intensity reach to 43% of its initial intensity. So, 0.20 mg/mL was used in following as the optimal concentration of Ag@AuNPs.

Upon addition of optimized concentration of Ag@AuNPs, the time-dependent fluorescence quenching of the CDs-Ap was investigated by monitoring changes of fluorescence intensity versus time. As it is shown in Fig. S5C and D, the maximum fluorescence quenching via Ag@AuNPs was resulted at 17 min. The fluorescence recovery process was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence signal in the presence of the optimum concentration of quencher (0.20 mg/mL) and Pb2+ ions (250 nM) in the different times. Fig. S6A and B shows that the fluorescence signal has been gradually increased to maximum up to 20 min and after this time, being constant. In the next steps, 20 min was considered as the incubation time.

The capability of biosensor for Pb2+ detection was investigated by monitoring the fluorescence signal after the addition of Pb2+ ions at different concentrations under optimal conditions. The addition of Pb2+ ions to the system induces detaching the CDs-Ap from Ag@AuNPs and increasing the fluorescence signal. Fig. 3 shows the increment of FL signal and recovery percent proportionally to Pb2+ concentration. The F/F0 intensity (F and F0 represented the FL intensity with and without Pb2+ions) was positively and linearly correlated with the concentration of Pb2+ ions. Recovery percent after addition Pb2+ ions reached around 60%. The linear ranges varied from 0.5 to 8 nM and 8 to 300 nM of Pb2+ ions with 0.2 nM as a detection limit (3 times the standard deviation of blank solutions divided by the slope). Rec.

The repeatability of the aptasensor was appraised by measuring the fluorescence intensity of 350 nM for five repetitions. The precision defined as relative standard deviation (RSD) was equal to 2.1 %. RSD exhibited good reproducibility. The stability of biosensor is an important aspect in the practical application which has been investigated by following response of the aptasensor to 250 nM of Pb2+. After 15 days, the fluorescence intensity remain 95% of its initial signal (Fig.S5).

Table 1 shows the comparison of the figures of merit between developed aptasensor and some previously reported fluorescent sensors for Pb2+ detection. It can be found that the detection limit is comparable or better than other methods.

Several heavy metal ions including Cu2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ with a concentration of 250 nM were used to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed biosensor. Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence changes induced by the other metal ions was negligible compared with the fluorescence induced by Pb2+. It was concluded that the proposed biosensor shows high selectivity toward Pb2+ because of the high affinity of the aptamer as recognition element.

To verify the reliability and applicability of biosensor, the Pb2+ amount of water and milk samples were measured. The sample were prepared as described in the experimental section. While no Pb2+ ions were detected in those samples, other experiments were performed by spiking at various concentrations of Pb2+. Results obtained in Table 2 display that the recoveries of spiked samples were ranged from 87 to 101% and RSD of 3.1–6.3% that indicating good accuracy. The trueness of the biosensor was examined by measuring the concentration of Pb2+ in the two real samples using atomic absorption spectroscopy. The result was reported in Table S1.

In summary, a new fluorescence biosensor has been introduced for Pb2+ detection using CDs as a fluorophore, the aptamer as a recognition element and Ag@AuNPs as the quencher. The results illustrate that the Ag@AuNPs with a good quenching efficiency was able to quench CDs conjugated to aptamer. The combination of CDs and aptamer for the selective fluorescence detection of Pb2+ has been minimally reported. The Pb2+ determination can be attained with several advantages including a low detection limit of 0.2 nM, good selectivity and stability.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Shahrekord University for the support of this work

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2023.116636.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
